coredata.dc:title: Engineering highly graphitic carbon quantum dots by catalytic dehydrogenation and carbonization of Ti3C2Tx-MXene wrapped polystyrene spheres
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Hao-Ran
Meng, Wei-Ming
Wang, Ri-Yuan
Chen, Fang-Lin
Li, Tao
Wang, Ding-Ding
Wang, Feng
Zhu, San-E
Wei, Chun-Xiang
Lu, Hong-Dian
Yang, Wei
coredata.dc:description: The catalytic dehydrogenation of Ti3C2Tx-MXene nanosheets induced by their surface terminating C-Ti-O group has attracted tremendous interests in the fabrication of unsaturated organics and carbon materials. Herein, the highly graphitic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by pyrolyzing cationic polystyrene (CPS) spheres which were wrapped by Ti3C2Tx-MXene nanosheets. The morphological features and fluorescent properties of the as-prepared CQDs were characterized and the growth mechanism of CQDs was proposed. In this unique hybrid model, Ti3C2Tx-MXene exhibited high efficiency in catalyzing dehydrogenation and carbonization of CPS to highly graphitic CQDs at a relatively low pyrolysis temperature of 410 °C. During the pyrolysis, Ti3C2Tx-MXene shell acted as a closed barrier to retain the volatilization of degradation products of CPS and promote their diffusion into the MXene interlayers. Simultaneously, lots of CPS degradation products were adhered to the surface of Ti3C2Tx-MXene nanosheets, where the dehydrogenation was carried out by the C-Ti-O catalytic active sites. Eventually, they were carbonized to highly graphitic CQDs with excellent photoluminescence behavior. This work provides a facile strategy to convert polystyrene matrix into graphitic carbon-based material, which helps to develop the catalytic carbonization mechanism of MXene/polymer nanocomposites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MXene
Polystyrene spheres
Catalytic dehydrogenation
Carbonization
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Catalyzing carbonization of CPS upon Ti3C2Tx-MXene
Morphological features of the resultant CQDs
Surface compositions and photoluminescence behavior of CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of experimental and theoretical TGA curves of CPS@MXene hybrid. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

FE-SEM images of (a) CPS and (b) CPS@MXene spheres, and (c) HSMX/CQDs hybrid. TEM images of (d) hollow spherical structure of HSMX/CQDs hybrid (inset shows the spherical walls) and (e) distribution of nanoparticles within HSMX/CQDs hybrid. (f) XRD patterns of Ti3C2Tx-MXene and HSMX/CQDs hybrid. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The as-prepared CQDs are characterized by (a) XRD pattern and (b) Raman spectrum. (c) TEM images of CQDs (inset shows the lattice fringes) and (d) the corresponding size distribution histogram in c. (e, f) 2D and 3D AFM topography images of CQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Surface chemical compositions of CQDs characterized by (a) FTIR spectrum and (b) XPS survey spectrum. High-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C1s and (d) O1s peaks. (e) UV–Vis spectrum and PL spectra of CQDs. (f) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs upon excitation at diverse wavelengths (insert: photographs of the solution of CQDs taken under visible light (left) and 312 nm UV light (right)). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Py-GC/MS profiles of decomposition products from CPS and CPS@MXene. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) XPS survey spectra of Ti3C2Tx-MXene, MXene-T and HSMX/CQDs. The corresponding surface compositions can be found in Fig. S3. High-resolution XPS spectra of the O1s peak for (b) MXene, (c) MXene-T, and (d) HSMX/CQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The schematic preparation route of CQDs via a sacrificial template method.
Possible catalytic dehydrogenation of CPS upon C-Ti-O catalytic sites of Ti3C2Tx-MXene.
Thermal degradation properties of CPS@MXene hybrid under nitrogen flow.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: T10%, temperature at which 10% degradation occurs; Tmax, temperature obtained from DTG (derivative TG curves) curves at which the maximum mass loss rate occurs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The reported data are averages of three measurements.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: No residual char was found in the porcelain boat, but a yellow tar-like substance was found to be attached to the inner wall of the quartz tube.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the characteristics of CQDs from different synthesis routes.

head.ce:title: Engineering highly graphitic carbon quantum dots by catalytic dehydrogenation and carbonization of Ti3C2Tx-MXene wrapped polystyrene spheres

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy, Materials and Chemical Engineering, Hefei University, Hefei, Anhui, 230601, People's Republic of China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Energy, Materials and Chemical Engineering, Hefei University, Hefei, Anhui, 230601, People's Republic of China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Hao-Ran Chen and Wei-Ming Meng contributed equally to this work (co-first author).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The catalytic dehydrogenation of Ti3C2Tx-MXene nanosheets induced by their surface terminating C-Ti-O group has attracted tremendous interests in the fabrication of unsaturated organics and carbon materials. Herein, the highly graphitic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by pyrolyzing cationic polystyrene (CPS) spheres which were wrapped by Ti3C2Tx-MXene nanosheets. The morphological features and fluorescent properties of the as-prepared CQDs were characterized and the growth mechanism of CQDs was proposed. In this unique hybrid model, Ti3C2Tx-MXene exhibited high efficiency in catalyzing dehydrogenation and carbonization of CPS to highly graphitic CQDs at a relatively low pyrolysis temperature of 410 °C. During the pyrolysis, Ti3C2Tx-MXene shell acted as a closed barrier to retain the volatilization of degradation products of CPS and promote their diffusion into the MXene interlayers. Simultaneously, lots of CPS degradation products were adhered to the surface of Ti3C2Tx-MXene nanosheets, where the dehydrogenation was carried out by the C-Ti-O catalytic active sites. Eventually, they were carbonized to highly graphitic CQDs with excellent photoluminescence behavior. This work provides a facile strategy to convert polystyrene matrix into graphitic carbon-based material, which helps to develop the catalytic carbonization mechanism of MXene/polymer nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted a great deal of attention due to their stable photoluminescence, low toxicity, excellent chemical stability and biocompatibility [1,2]. Since CQDs were found from arc-discharged soot in the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes [3], numerous strategies for CQDs synthesis have been explored, which can be categorized as two kinds of routes, “top-down” (e.g. laser ablation [4], chemical/electrochemical oxidation [5,6]) and “bottom-up” (e.g. microwave irradiation [7], pyrolysis [8], hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment [9,10]). Among bottom-up approaches, direct pyrolysis of organics or polymers is a promising option to synthesize CQDs via dehydration and carbonization, because of its prominent advantages, such as low cost, easy operation, solvent-free, precursor selection voluminous and large-scale production [11]. Many organics, including citric acid [12], glycerol [13], acetic acid [14], glucose [15] and ethylenediamine-tetraacetic acid salts [16], are used as carbon precursors to fabricate CQDs via pyrolysis without any solvents. Compared to small organics, one-step pyrolysis of polymers to prepare CQDs is more complicated, because pyrolysis often occurs at high temperatures (500–800 °C) and the degraded products are difficult to be controlled precisely [17–19]. On the other hand, the generation of graphitic carbon via dehydrogenation and carbonization without any assistance of catalysts or extreme condition is almost unattainable during the thermal degradation of polymers [20]. It is therefore a challenge to generate CQDs by direct pyrolysis of polymers using suitable catalysts at low temperatures.

Layered two-dimensional (2D) crystalline carbides and/or nitrides, MXenes (primarily Ti3C2Tx-MXene), have attracted tremendous interests recently [21–23]. Ti3C2Tx-MXene nanosheets are commonly obtained through a chemical liquid etching method, which leads to the generation of many functional groups such as –OH, –F, –O, and thus endowing strong interfacial catalytic activity [24,25]. For example, Diao et al. reported that the C-Ti-O terminating groups on the Ti3C2Tx-MXene surface could promote the catalytic dehydrogenation of ethylbenzene [26]. Liu et al. demonstrated the dehydrogenation kinetics in which the in-situ formed metallic Ti facilitated the dissociation and recombination of molecular hydrogen on the interface of Ti3C2Tx-MXene nanosheets [27]. Slot et al. utilized Ti3C2Tx-MXene as an acid catalyst for the dehydration of adjacent hydroxyl groups in polymer skeleton via terminated –OH and metallic/non-metallic element facilitating the dehydrogenation of C–H bonds to form unsaturated structure [28].

Undoubtedly, a large number of styrene derivatives, which are similar to the structural units of graphitic carbon-based materials, can be produced in the thermal decomposition of polymers including polystyrene (PS) and acrylonitrile-butadiene-styrene (ABS) etc. However, the self-carbonization process is difficult to accomplish, because the decomposition products are prone to be oxidized or gasified when the temperature is higher than 400 °C and hard to be deposited on the substrate to form char. Interestingly, in addition to dehydrogenation, Ti3C2Tx-MXene has exhibited a strong catalytic carbonization effect during the combustion of polymer/MXene nanocomposites [29–33], which has been demonstrated by that the incorporation of Ti3C2Tx-MXene into those polymers of PS and ABS can improve the char residues with enhanced graphitization via the catalytic carbonization. Therefore, Ti3C2Tx-MXene shows great potential as a highly-active catalyst for hydrocarbon dehydrogenation and charring reaction.

In light of the aforementioned, this study aimed to develop a non-traditional approach for the synthesis of CQDs through direct pyrolysis of polystyrene/Ti3C2Tx-MXene composite without additional catalysts at a low temperature to obtain a high graphitic degree. The highly graphitic CQDs were synthesized via a sacrificial template method by pyrolysis of Ti3C2Tx-MXene encapsulated CPS spheres at a relatively low temperature of 410 °C. The morphology, structure and fluorescent properties of the as-prepared CQDs were investigated, and a possible mechanism of catalytic dehydrogenation and carbonization of polystyrene matrix into CQDs in the presence of Ti3C2Tx-MXene was proposed. Based on the hollow spherical micro-reactors constructed by Ti3C2Tx-MXene nanosheets, the degraded products of CPS were efficiently trapped on the surface and entered into the interlayer of Ti3C2Tx-MXene. Subsequently, they participated in the carbonization reactions triggered by the interfacial catalytic dehydrogenation of C-Ti-O groups, leading to the growth of CQDs on the catalytic sites of Ti3C2Tx-MXene.

Preparation of CQDs: The Ti3C2Tx-MXene wrapped cationic polystyrene (CPS) microspheres (CPS@MXene) at a CPS to MXene mass ratio of 3/1 were firstly fabricated based on the electrostatic interaction between CPS and Ti3C2Tx-MXene. The hollow spherical Ti3C2Tx-MXene/CQDs hybrid (HSMX/CQDs) was obtained by thermal annealing of CPS@MXene spheres at 410 °C for 2 h under flowing nitrogen via sacrificing CPS templates. A schematic of the preparation procedure of CQDs is illustrated in Scheme 1 . Detailed information on the delamination of Ti3C2Tx-MXene and the synthesis of HSMX/CQDs hybrid can be found in the Supporting Information.

Purification of CQDs: The HSMX/CQDs hybrid was dispersed in o-dichlorobenzene (DCB) with ultrasonic agitation for 20 min then purified by silica gel column chromatography using DCB as the eluent to remove HSMX. The collected eluent was subsequently evaporated by reduced pressure at 70 °C to get a brown black powder. The powder was further washed with a mixed solvent consisting of acetone and petroleum ether by centrifugation for three times then vacuum dried at 60 °C for 24h to obtain the purified CQDs.

Morphology and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) of the samples were observed on a TESCAN MIRA field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM). High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL JEM 2100PLUS) were employed to distinguish the crystal structure and particle size of the samples. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Co.) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.1542 nm). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out to investigate the sample surface chemical composition and bonding states using a Nexsa spectrometer (Thermo Fisher) equipped with an Al Kα excitation radiation (hν = 1486.6 eV). Raman spectra were recorded from 100 to 2000 cm−1 on a DXR laser confocal micro Raman spectrometer (Thermo Fisher). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was conducted on a DI Multimode V scanning probe microscope (Bruker) in tapping mode. The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence properties were recorded via an absorption spectrometer (UV-1800, Mapada) and a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh FLS1000, EI) at room temperature, respectively. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a TG 209 F1 thermo-analyzer instrument at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under nitrogen condition. The degradation products of CPS and CPS@MXene spheres were monitored using a gas chromatography-mass spectrometer (GCMS-QP2010Ultra). 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were measured by a Bruker AVANCE Ⅲ 400 MHZ spectrometer. The quantum yield (QY) was measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (FP-8500, JASCO). The CQDs yield value was calculated according to the following equation [34]:
CQDs
yield% =
mass
of
C
in
purified
CQDs
mass
of
C
in
CPS
of
CPS@MXene
× 100 %
Where the C element was determined by EDS. Elemental analysis results showed that the C content in CQDs and CPS were 82.2 and 82.3 wt%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalyzing carbonization of CPS upon Ti3C2Tx-MXene
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of Ti3C2Tx-MXene as a catalyst on the carbonization of CPS was initially investigated. Fig. 1 shows the thermal degradation of Ti3C2Tx-MXene, CPS and CPS@MXene; the corresponding TGA data are listed in Table 1 . It can be seen that Ti3C2Tx-MXene exhibits high thermal stability. The CPS degrades almost completely at a temperature higher than 400 °C and leaves only 0.4 wt% char at 600 °C. While for the CPS@MXene hybrid, it shows superior thermal stability and achieves a higher residual char yield. The TGA curve of the theoretical data for the CPS@MXene hybrid was calculated by linear combinations of the experimental TGA curves of single components of CPS and MXene, that is, massCPS@MXene-Cal = 3/4 × massCPS+1/4 × massMXene. It exhibits that the theoretical char (Char-cal) yield is around 24.5 wt%. However, the hybrid leaves 34.4 wt% experimental residual char (Char-exp) at 600 °C with an increase of 40.4 wt% compared to the calculated value. Similarly, the experimental char of CPS@MXene remained in tube furnace after thermal annealing is 30.2 wt%, whereas the theoretical yield is 22.6 wt%. The results reveal that the Ti3C2Tx-MXene wrapped on CPS spheres efficiently promotes the carbonization of CPS during thermal degradation.

The morphology and microstructure evolution of CPS@MXene microspheres before and after thermal annealing was observed by FE-SEM and TEM images. The FE-SEM images show that the CPS@MXene spheres are around 775 nm in diameter with a much rougher surface due to the encapsulation of Ti3C2Tx-MXene nanosheets, when compared with that of the 760 nm CPS spheres (Fig. 2 a and b). Meanwhile, the extra Ti3C2Tx-MXene nanosheets that do not participate in the microencapsulation served as “bridges” to connect the adjacent spheres to form a clustered spherical structure. A macroporous architecture composed of hollow spherical Ti3C2Tx-MXene (HSMX) was then achieved by pyrolyzing CPS from the CPS@MXene spheres (Fig. 2c and d). As measured directly, the inserted TEM image in Fig. 2d demonstrates that the spherical walls with a thickness of around 20 nm are composed of 10–20 layers of Ti3C2Tx-MXene nanosheets. The flexible Ti3C2Tx-MXene nanosheets are curled and stacked in a staggered manner accompanied by a large number of nanoparticles, as indicated in Fig. 2e. Noticeably, the nanoparticles, perhaps CQDs, are distributed on their surfaces and also inserted into their interlayers. In addition, the crystal structure of HSMX/CQDs determined by XRD shows that the intrinsic (002) peak of Ti3C2Tx-MXene initially at 2θ = 7.3° obviously shifts to a smaller angle of around 2θ = 6.9° with an increase of corresponding d-spacing to around 1.28 nm (Fig. 2f). This is in good agreement with the TEM observation, which is primarily attributed to the fact that CQDs have been inserted into the interlayer of Ti3C2Tx-MXene thereby increasing their interlayer distance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological features of the resultant CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The resultant CQDs were purified from HSMX/CQDs hybrid and the final yield of CQDs was 8.0%. The morphology and microstructure of the as-prepared CQDs were elucidated by XRD, Raman, HRTEM and AFM. The XRD pattern of CQDs shown in Fig. 3 a displays a diffraction peakat 24.5° with a d-spacing of around 0.36 nm, which corresponds to the typical graphitic (002) plane [35,36]. Fig. 3b shows the Raman spectrum which can provide the crystallinity information of CQDs. It is apparent that the disordered-induced D-band and the graphite G-band appear in 1370 and 1600 cm−1, respectively; and a weak 2D-band peak related to the sp2 bonded carbon atoms in a graphite layer is observed at 2770 cm−1 [37,38]. Moreover, the intensity ratio of D- and G-bands (ID/IG) of the CQDs is 0.70 (Fig. S1), which is much lower than most of the reports as listed in Table 2 [19,39–48]. The results of XRD and Raman therefore indicate that the as-prepared CQDs possess a high graphitization degree.

The HRTEM image in Fig. 3c shows that the CQDs are quasi-spherical and mono-dispersed. Meanwhile, the lattice fringes display that the CQDs have an inner-plane spacing of 0.35 ± 0.03 nm corresponding to the (002) graphitic planes, which is perfectly consistent with the XRD result. By statistical analysis of 200 nanoparticles in Fig. 3c, it demonstrates that the particle size distribution of CQD is in a narrow range of 0.5–4.0 nm and their average particle size is around 1.8 nm (Fig. 3d). Fig. 3e illustrates the 2D AFM image of CQDs which are randomly distributed on the substrate. Accordingly, the 3D AFM image (Fig. 3f) reveals that the height of CQDs is in a range of 0.5–7.5 nm, implying that most of them consist of 2–11 graphite-like layers (based on the thickness of a monolayer of graphite is 0.3–0.4 nm) [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface compositions and photoluminescence behavior of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of the surface chemical compositions of CQDs on photoluminescence (PL) behavior was investigated by FTIR and XPS. Fig. 4 a shows the FTIR spectrum of CQDs. It is noteworthy that there is no C-H stretching vibration absorption that can be found at 2700–3000 cm−1, suggesting the absence of aromatic hydrogen. The phenomenon is consistent with the result of 1H NMR analysis (Fig. S2), in which no signals is corresponding to the aromatic hydrogen (∼7.16 ppm), except for the signals at 6.5–8.0 ppm of C6H4Cl2 hydrogen and 1.4 ppm of H2O hydrogen [50,51]. On the other hand, a range of absorption bands appear at 3400–3750, 1690–1750, 1510–1650 and 1458 cm−1, which are assigned to the -OH stretching, C=O stretching, C=C stretching, and O-H bending vibrations, respectively [47,52]. The FTIR and NMR results suggest that the H elements in the aromatic structure of CQDs have been almost completely removed during thermal annealing, possibly due to the strong catalytic dehydrogenation of Ti3C2Tx-MXene. However, the inevitable slight oxidation leads to the generation of -OH and -C=O groups on the marginal region of CQDs [53].

In the XPS spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 4b), two prominent features of the C1s and O1s peaks are clearly observed, indicating that CQDs are primarily composed of C and O elements. The content of C and O is 82.2% and 17.8% respectively, confirming the dominant role of carbon molecules. As shown in Fig. 4c, the C1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three C-containing groups, including C-C/C=C (assignment to graphitic carbon structure, 284.3 eV), C-O/C-OH (286.1 eV) and O-C=O (288.5 eV), respectively [54–56]. In particular, the C-C/C=C groups in CQDs account for 77.8% of the total carbon species; therefore, this high proportion contributes to the high degree of graphitization of CQDs. Meanwhile, the two deconvoluted peaks in O1s spectra (Fig. 4d) assigned to the C-O/C-OH and O-C=O bonds, respectively, further suggest the introduction of oxygen-containing functional groups to CQDs, which is beneficial to endowing CQDs with optical activity [57,58].

The UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the ethanol solution of the as-prepared CQDs were therefore studied at room temperature. The UV–vis spectrum shown in Fig. 4e displays that there are obvious absorption peaks at 270 and 320 nm, which are attributed to the π-π∗ transition of aromatic C=C bonds and the n-π∗ transition of C=O, respectively (Fig. 4e) [45,59]. As illustrated in the photographs inserted in Fig. 4f, the ethanol solution of CQDs is almost colorless and transparent in daylight, but it emits an intense yellow emission under the excitation of 312 nm UV radiation. In addition, the CQDs exhibit a typical excitation wavelength-dependent PL behavior, and the PL spectra tend to shift from blue to red over the excitation wavelength range of 300–440 nm with a quantum yield (QY) of 25.63%, as shown in Fig. 4f. This indicates that the CQDs prepared herein without passivation possess excellent fluorescence properties [58].

In order to clarify the influence of the CPS@MXene hybrid model on the pyrolysis synthesis of CQDs, the Py-GC/MS measurements were carried out to differentiate the degradation processes from CPS and CPS@MXene. Thermal degradation of polystyrene is generally considered to be initiated by chain-end cleavage and random cleavage, and the latter is favorable for the formation of toluene and α-methylstyrene [60]. Fig. 5 shows the Py-GC/MS profiles of pristine CPS and CPS@MXene; the corresponding gas degradation products are summarized in Table S1. The CPS produced a large amount of styrene (58.1%) but small amounts of toluene (1.10%) and α-methylstyrene (0.76%). In comparison, CPS@MXene hybrid produces more toluene (1.81%) and α-methylstyrene (4.59%), while the content of styrene decreased dramatically to 29.4%. It therefore can be speculated that in the CPS@MXene hybrid model, the Ti3C2Tx-MXene nanosheets wrapped on CPS spheres facilitate the random cleavage of CPS. Two possible reasons may be accounted for this change. One is that the deprivation of H from PS backbone catalyzed by the C-Ti-O catalytic sites of Ti3C2Tx-MXene is beneficial to initiating the random cleavage as illustrated in Scheme 2 . The other is that the hollow nano-spherical structure promotes the insertion of degradation products into the MXene interlayers and improves their interactions with C-Ti-O groups. Furthermore, it can be summarized from Table S1 that the CPS@MXene hybrid produced more polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) degradation products than the pristine CPS. For example, the yield of naphthalene derivates from CPS@MXene is 10.94%, while that from CPS is only 0.13%. It implies that the degradation products of CPS can also be dehydrogenated by the C-Ti-O groups, subsequently inducing the cyclization and cross-linking reactions among them to form PAHs, which is conducive to the carbonization reactions [61].

Supplementary, the evolution of C-Ti-O groups of Ti3C2Tx-MXene, MXene-T (thermal treated pristine Ti3C2Tx-MXene) and HSMX/CQDs during thermal annealing was analyzed by XPS (Fig. 6 ). The high resolution O1s spectra of them reveal four peaks (Fig. 6b–d), which represent the bonds of Ti-O (∼530.1 eV), C-Ti-O (∼531.3 eV), C-Ti-OH (∼532.5 eV) and activated oxygen species (∼533.7 eV) [26,56], respectively. Noticeably, the C-Ti-O groups in MXene-T account for 38.94% of the total oxygen species, which is higher than that either in MXene (27.85%) or in HSMX/CQDs (19.44%). One can speculate that a high proportion of C-Ti-O groups have been activated during thermal annealing, which is helpful to catalyze the dehydrogenation of both CPS and its degradation products. The decreased C-Ti-O groups in HSMX/CQDs hybrid may be attributed to the occupation of char (mainly CQDs) on those catalytic sites. That is estimated by the HSMX/CQDs hybrid has the highest atomic content of C1s (53.79%), while 45.66% for MXene-T and 35.81% for MXene (Fig. S3).

Based on the discussion above, a possible mechanism of synthesis of CQDs by catalytic dehydrogenation and carbonization of CPS@MXene was proposed, as illustrated in Scheme 1. The hollow nano-spherical HSMXs can be imagined as micro-reactors, which efficiently retain the degradation products of CPS in their nano-scale enclosed space, thus generating high pressure inside. This significantly facilitates the close contact between polystyrene backbones and C-Ti-O groups on Ti3C2Tx-MXene surface, thereby catalyzing the dehydrogenation of CPS into unsaturated structures with aromatic rings at a relatively low temperature of 410 °C. The pyrolysis products were then pressed into the interlayer of Ti3C2Tx-MXene and anchored on the active sites of C-Ti-O groups to undergo catalytic dehydrogenation and cyclization reaction, which provided a possibility of inducing cross-linking reactions among them to form PAHs. More important, PAHs can serve as planar platforms to trap more small aromatic hydrocarbons with alkene bonds at the end structures (such as styrene and its derivates) for carbonization, and then gradually form larger planar structures. Finally, highly graphitic CQDs were assembled by stacking 2–11 graphite-like layers.

In summary, the quasi-spherical highly graphitic CQDs with excellent fluorescent properties were facilely synthesized by thermal evaporating CPS templates from CPS@MXene spheres. It is noteworthy that the hollow nano-spherical Ti3C2Tx-MXene structure exhibits an important influence on thermal degradation process of CPS, resulting in the retention of pyrolysis volatiles inside the hollow spheres and their diffusion into the interlayer of spherical Ti3C2Tx-MXene shells. The surface terminating C-Ti-O groups of Ti3C2Tx-MXene could not only catalyze dehydrogenation of CPS and its degradation products, but also promote the carbonization of degradation products into CQDs. The development of the synthesis of CQDs via catalytic dehydrogenation and carbonization of polystyrene matrix upon Ti3C2Tx-MXene is of great significance for expanding its application fields such as catalytic carbonization of polymeric materials and photoluminescence materials.

Hao-Ran Chen: Writing – original draft, preparation, Resources, Conceptualization. Wei-Ming Meng: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Ri-Yuan Wang: Software, Formal analysis. Fang-Lin Chen: Investigation, Data curation. Tao Li: Validation, Data curation. Ding-Ding Wang: Experimental, Data curation. Feng Wang: Validation, Data curation. San-E Zhu: Writing – review & editing. Chun-Xiang Wei: Writing – review & editing. Hong-Dian Lu: Supervision, Methodology, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Wei Yang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by Anhui Provincial Key Technologies R&D Program (1804a09020070 and 202004a05020044), China Scholarship Council (202008340021), Startup Fund for Distinguished Scholars in Hefei University (20RC37), Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation (2108085QB47) and Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholar (2008085J26).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2022.01.028.
